California Stewardship Alliancecaliforniastewardship.org
The Context
Eight Pressures Facing California Communities
01
Fire Safety & Evacuation
Density mandates in high-hazard zones increase wildfire exposure. Many communities lack road capacity to safely evacuate the populations that mandated growth would add.
02
Traffic & Mobility
Trades, construction, healthcare, and service workers depend on vehicles to do their jobs — not just reach them. Mandates add population without proportional road capacity.
03
Parking
State law eliminates minimum parking requirements near transit. Cities absorbing mandated density are left managing spillover onto streets and neighborhoods not built for it.
04
Infrastructure Capacity
Water, sewer, roads, schools, and hospitals are sized for current populations. Mandated densification stresses systems that cities — not the state — must fund and maintain.
05
Housing Affordability
Upzoning historically triggers land speculation and displacement. Market-rate densification has not reliably produced units accessible to median-income California households.
06
Local Equity & Outside Capital
Large-scale rezoning creates arbitrage for institutional investors. Community-built equity and local ownership can be extracted at scale — wealth leaves the neighborhood.
07
Liability
The state mandates housing in unsafe or underprepared locations — but when something goes wrong, the legal and financial liability falls on cities and counties, not Sacramento.
08
Community Character & Quality of Life
One-size mandates override locally adopted general plans and the democratic processes behind them. California's communities differ in scale, history, and identity for good reason.
"These are not objections to growth — they are requests that growth be grounded in present-day evidence, not planning assumptions from a prior era."
California Stewardship Alliancecaliforniastewardship.org
The Problem — Evacuation Capacity
The Canyon Has One Road Out.
Physical Reality
1
Lane out of the canyon
Evacuation geometry is fixed by terrain. No zoning law, state mandate, or legislative amendment changes the lane count. This is engineering, not opinion.
→
Legislative Response
0
of 132 bills require evacuation analysis
Of every housing bill tracked in the 2026 session, not one requires a community to assess evacuation capacity before a density mandate takes effect.
→
The Conclusion
Every
mandate
that ignores this is built on a fiction
Sacramento is mandating growth into communities it has never measured. When fire comes, the liability falls on cities — not the legislature that ordered them to grow.
"This is not an opinion. It is a measurement. And no mandate changes it."
The Problem
The Definition
of Insanity.
California has passed 275+ housing laws since 1982. The problem got worse every decade.
1982
Housing Accountability Act. "Prevent cities from blocking compliant housing." First of many.
2×
Median home prices doubled over the same period the laws were accumulating.
0
Change in homeownership rate over 30 years of mandates, amendments, and overhauls.
+132
More bills in the 2026 pipeline. Session not over. The cycle continues.
"The patches tell you where it breaks. Enough patches, and they tell you why."
California Stewardship Alliancecaliforniastewardship.org

Every Law Tells Communities to Grow.

MANDATE APPLIES
502 California jurisdictions received RHNA mandates — not one assessed for physical capacity first.
California Stewardship Alliancecaliforniastewardship.org
The Model — Paris, 1993
Dense. Walkable. Transit on every corner.
40 sq miles. 2.1 million people. 300+ Metro stations.
Flat terrain. No fire season. Centuries of density.
Every road leads to another road.
The Application — California, 2025
Canyon roads. Dry hills. One way out.
163,000 sq miles. 39 million people. Minimal rail.
Mountains, canyons, desert, coast. Annual fire seasons.
Many roads lead to one road out.
Sacramento Is Planning for 1995.
California spans the geography of most of Europe — yet one academic framework, built for Paris, is being applied to every canyon, hillside, and farm town in the state.
"The theory was built for Paris. The cobblestones don't fit. The canyons don't evacuate. The fires don't care about density bonuses."
The Unholy Alliance

The Framework
Capacity First.Then Growth.
1
Measure
Assess carrying capacity — fixed physical constraints + expandable systems
2
Invest
State funds water, transit, schools, emergency services first
3
Grow
Communities develop within their capacity — ownership-format housing
4
Mature
Corridors absorb a final, measured increment of infill development
5
Propagate
Overflow becomes the seed capital for the next community
"This is how America built itself for 250 years. We're not inventing something new. We're restoring something that worked."
californiastewardship.org
The Answer
Five Measurable Constraints. Safety Comes First.
Primary Constraint
Evacuation
Geometry
Lane count & road grade
Intersection spacing
Merge point analysis
Maximum clearance rate
"When the fire is 2 miles out, how many minutes does one more unit add to clearance time?"
ΔT
Marginal minutes added
per proposed project
Buildable Land
Total acreage assessed
Minus fire hazard zones
Minus floodplains & slopes
Yields a hard number
Water & Sewer
Infrastructure headroom
Treatment capacity
Distribution limits
Expandable with investment
Schools & Services
Enrollment capacity
Emergency coverage
Response time modeling
Expandable with investment
Energy & Grid
Grid connection capacity
Renewable integration
Microgrid potential
Designed for 2026 needs
Not political judgments. Engineering measurements — grounded in NIST, HCM 2022, and NFPA 101. Any resident, researcher, or auditor can reproduce them.
California Stewardship Alliancecaliforniastewardship.org
The Technology
JOSH — Jurisdictional Objective Standards for Housing
Proof of Concept.Real Results.
Drop any proposed project into any California community.
In seconds, using public federal data, it grades evacuation capacity.
Live result · Encinitas AB 747
Sage Canyon Apartments
120 units · New Encinitas
48.64
min added to
evacuation clearance
Limit: 2.25 min  ·  21.6× over threshold
DISCRETIONARY
Grounded in NIST, HCM 2022 & NFPA 101. Every number reproducible by any licensed engineer.
Open source, AGPL licensed. Free to any city, resident, or researcher.
Deployed in Encinitas under a CSA MOU. City Council agenda item in motion.
Live Demo

California Stewardship Alliancecaliforniastewardship.org
The Answer
How Capital Flows
State invests first.
Growth follows.
Step 1
State Measures & Invests
HCD certifies capacity. State funds roads, water, sewer, transit — before any growth mandate.
↓
Step 2
Local Builders Enter First
Capacity zones unlock CEQA streamlining — no institutional capital barrier, no entitlement lottery.
↓
Step 3
Ownership-Format Housing
Townhomes, duplexes, small-lot homes. Working families own — not rent. Equity builds locally.
↓
Step 4
Land Value Rises
State investment uplifts property values. Incremental tax revenue — that didn't exist before — is captured.
Where the Money Goes
The Value Capture Loop
Self-sustaining. No new taxes.
The Engine
Incremental property tax from new development — captured, not given away to developers.
Under RHNA, the State mandates upzoning and developers capture the uplift. This inverts that: the State creates the value, the community keeps it.
40%
City Capacity Dividend
Unrestricted general fund — no strings, no application. Scales with growth.
30%
Affordable Housing
Within the same community. Structural, not aspirational. Scales with every unit built.
20%
State Repayment
Infrastructure investment returns to the State — funding the next community's capacity.
10%
Administration
Program oversight, methodology maintenance, certification operations.
The growth pays for itself — and funds its own affordability.

California Stewardship Alliancecaliforniastewardship.org
The Ballot Initiative
Balanced Housing Growth Management Act  ·  Proposed Amendment to the California Constitution
"The State of California may not impose housing growth mandates on any community without first measuring its physical carrying capacity and investing in infrastructure to create that capacity."
The Inversion
State Invests Before It Mandates
Under RHNA, developers capture the windfall, cities absorb the cost, residents bear the risk. This reverses all three — the State creates value through investment, the community keeps it.
30%
Affordable Housing — Locked In
30% of incremental property tax from new development funds affordable housing within the same community. Constitutional floor. No new taxes. The growth pays for its own affordability.
40%
City Capacity Dividend
Cities that certify capacity and enable development earn an unrestricted 40% dividend. No application. No strings. It scales with growth — making participation the obvious rational choice.
Done
Cities That Build Out Are Finished
When a community fills its certified capacity, it is mature — no further mandates. The State's obligation shifts to building capacity elsewhere. Neighborhoods protected by engineering, not politics.
The Erie Canal. The transcontinental railroad. California's own aqueducts. Growth has always followed investment. We are not inventing something new — we are restoring something that worked.
Sections 4–8 require a vote of the People to change
California Stewardship Alliancecaliforniastewardship.org

The Ask
What Your Investment Does
Each level funds a specific, measurable outcome. Your commitment tonight has a direct address.
i
Community Defender
$5,000
Funds neighborhood advocacy resources and legal strategy support for one community facing an immediate threat.
ii
Regional Steward
$10,000
Funds one full city government engagement — legal strategy, planning tools, and municipal support for one jurisdiction.
iii
Founding Ally
$25,000
Funds one full legislative district campaign — candidate research, voter outreach, and accountability tracking.
iv
Lead Steward
$50,000+
Funds Phase 2 statewide expansion — new district engagements, expanded research capacity, and the Balanced Housing Framework constitutional groundwork.
Giving levels reflected on the inside of your place card
